title: Business Maturity Model — Stage 4: Focus
description: Canonical source for the Focus stage of The Business Maturity Model. Structured content (snapshot, big picture, the 8 departments grouped under 3 functions, the Start-Here three, success criteria, CTA, sources). Renders to the web diagnostic and the branded PDF. Living source, update over time.
name: Focus
tagline: You're past survival. Now the business is pulling you ten directions at once, and working harder isn't fixing it.
cover_foot: A read on where you are, where you're stuck, and what to fix next.

snapshot.who: 5 to 9 people. You manage the work now instead of doing all of it.
snapshot.constraint: You're trying to be everything to everyone. That's why you feel scattered.
snapshot.graduate_by: Niching down. More of your best-fit customers, fewer of everyone else.

big_picture: The early stages were about survival. Make the first sale, get steady customers, hire your first people. You did it. Now you've got a small team and money coming in, and a new problem shows up. You took every kind of customer and every kind of job to get here, and now the business pulls you in ten directions at once. You're busy every single day and it still feels like you're spinning.

The longer you wait, the more the business gets built around the mess. You add systems to handle work you never should have taken, and hire people who only know their corner of it. Focus only gets harder from here, which is why this is the stage to take seriously now.

Working harder won't fix this stage. The businesses that break through get specific about who they serve and what they sell, and they let the rest go. Here's what that looks like across every part of your business.
thesis: The stage comes down to one decision: who you're for, and what you'll stop doing. The next pages run that decision across all three engines of your business.
overview_intro: Every business runs on three engines. Eight parts sit inside them. At Focus, one part is breaking each engine right now. Those three are marked Start here. Get them right and the rest follows.

departments.name: Marketing
departments.tell: A hundred leads come in and you'd take any of them.
departments.constraint: You're drowning in leads that aren't a fit. Plenty of inquiries come in, but they don't turn into much money.
departments.do_this: Make your marketing more specific. Pick the one customer you just decided to build for and write to their problem in their words, so the wrong-fit leads rule themselves out before they ever call. Ten right-fit leads you can close beat a hundred tire-kickers who eat your week.

departments.name: Sales
departments.constraint: Deals slip and you can't see why. Nobody's tracking the funnel.
departments.do_this: Put your numbers on one weekly sheet, by person: leads, demos, closes, cash. Read down the funnel to where you fall off. The two that decide this stage are speed-to-lead and show rate.

departments.name: Product & service
departments.tell: You quote a $500 job and a $50,000 job the same way.
departments.constraint: You said yes to everyone to get here, so you deliver ten slightly different things and none of them is the best at anything.
departments.do_this: Pull your customer list and find the 20% who pay the most and complain the least. Build your best version of the business for them, and stop bending the offer for everyone else. That's the Hedgehog. Be the best at one thing instead of decent at ten.

departments.name: Customer service
departments.constraint: You can't see who's happy and who's about to walk.
departments.do_this: Get a basic CRM and track who you keep, who you lose, who refers. The customer who slips away quietly costs you more than the one who complains, because you never got the chance to save them.

departments.name: Technology
departments.constraint: The team's on scattered tools, personal logins, and notes in five places.
departments.do_this: Get everyone on the same platforms. One place to talk, one to track work, a password manager so nothing lives in one person's head. Nothing fancy, just shared.

departments.name: Finance
departments.tell: One slow-paying customer changes your whole month.
departments.constraint: Cash is lumpy. Some months are flush, some are tight, and one big bill can knock you sideways, because nobody's looking more than a few weeks out.
departments.benchmark: Healthy here is 10 to 15% pretax profit, with two months of operating expenses sitting in cash.
departments.do_this: Stand up a P&L and a cash flow forecast so you can see the money coming and going weeks ahead. The number that runs this stage is gross profit per labor dollar, what each dollar of payroll earns back. At five to nine people, payroll is your biggest cost and your biggest lever.

departments.name: Hiring
departments.constraint: You hire the best interview, then wonder where that person went.
departments.do_this: Hire to a scorecard. Write the outcomes the role must produce before you meet anyone, interview against it, and call their references. That's the A-Method. A bad hire at five people hurts more than at fifty.

departments.name: Your people
departments.constraint: Nobody wrote the rules down, so everyone runs their own.
departments.do_this: Write the basics down: time off, conduct, how things work here. Then get each person clear on what they're there to contribute. Lencioni calls the first part creating clarity; Drucker the second, focusing on contribution.

on_track: You can name your best 20% of customers
Your marketing speaks to one kind of person
You track your sales numbers, even by hand
You've got a P&L and a rough cash forecast
needs_work: You still take every customer who'll pay
You couldn't show last month's profit on one page
One big bill still scares you
You're the only one who knows how anything works
Every decision still runs through you

graduating: You'll know you're ready when you've niched down and it's working: better-fit customers, steadier cash, a team that runs without you in every call. The next stage is about giving those good customers something else to buy.

cta.headline: Where are you taking this business?
cta.body: Book a call and tell me. I'll map the numbers that get you there and the one or two moves that would change the most this year.
